figures.caption: Table 1: DLE with various samplers improves performance compared to stochastic self-consistency. Performance (maj@k and pass@k) of various methods on GSM8K, Humaneval and MMLU-Pro with Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct with parameters given in Section 4. Values are left blank when the method is not applicable to the metric. .
Table 5: Performance (maj@k and pass@k) of various methods on GSM8K, Humaneval and MMLU-Pro with the Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model.
Table 6: Performance (maj@k and pass@k) of various methods on GSM8K, Humaneval and MMLU-Pro with the Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct model.

Figure 6: Left: The per sequence log-probabilities are higher in DLE (ε-sampling) compared to self-consistency. Right: Sequences with log-probability ≥ −100 are more accurate for DLE (ε-sampling) compared to self-consistency.
Table 7: Performance (maj@k and pass@k) of various methods on GSM8K, Humaneval and MMLU-Pro with the Llama3.2-1B-Instruct.
Table 8: Performance (maj@k and pass@k) of various methods on GSM8K, Humaneval and MMLU-Pro with the Llama3.2-3B-Instruct model.

Figure 15: Inference time and performance for DLE with ε-sampling, measured using vLLM. At higher ε, inference time is shorter than the baseline. The performance boost is also more obvious since far fewer tokens are generated.

Figure 14: DLE with ε-sampling outperforms ε-sampling with far fewer new tokens generated. Number of new tokens is counted on average for each question. ε is marked with colors indicating its value. Stochastic self-consistency generates many more tokens to achieve a similar performance as DLE.

Figure 8: Left: DLE (ε-sampling) outperforms ε-sampled self-consistency with far fewer new tokens generated. The number of new tokens is averaged over k = 32 for each question. ε-sampling generates many more tokens for similar performance as compared to DLE. ε is colored according to its value. See Section 4.2. Right: For a fixed token budget, DLE achieves a higher accuracy and generates more sequences compared to ε-sampling.

Figure 9: Left: Cache rate is larger for DLE (ε-sampling) than ε-sampling. SGLang (k = 8) retrieves prefix cache efficiently such that the actual cache hit rate is close to the theoretical one. See Section 4.3. Right: Higher ε results in much lower runtime for DLE (ε-sampling) than ε-sampling at k = 32. The time benefit is the most significant at small batch sizes and high ε. Higher ε enables more prefix reuse and larger runtime benefits. See Section 4.3.
Table 3: The early-stopped branches result in identical final answers with high probability, motivating the addition of early-stopping rule, with low cost of additional compute.
Table 2: Adding an early-stopping condition to DLE (ε-sampling) baselines generally improves performance. Alternative branching algorithms DFS and GLOBALPROB yield worse performance.
